title: Black Sea fixture: one ship, three rationalities
title_ru: Черноморская сделка: одно судно, три рациональности

vessel.name: MV Northern Light
vessel.note: Fictional training vessel, not a real vessel record.

briefing: You are acting as the commercial manager. The vessel is opening in the Black Sea with limited bunker margin, a cautious captain and an owner watching cash. Your job is not to be heroic. Your job is to keep the vessel employed without creating a claim that destroys the voyage.
briefing_ru: Вы играете коммерческого менеджера. Судно открывается в Черном море, бункер ограничен, капитан осторожен, владелец смотрит на кассу. Задача не геройствовать, а занять судно без претензии, которая съест рейс.

steps.title: Which cargo lead do you chase first?
steps.prompt: Three brokers call within twenty minutes. The high-rate cargo is attractive, but the laycan and receiver are vague.

options.label: Take the steel coils lead at a moderate rate
options.consequence: You keep the ship employed with cleaner counterparties and less drama. The owner asks why the rate is not higher.

options.label: Push the high-rate grain cargo
options.consequence: The headline freight is strong, but the uncertainty moves risk from the fixture desk to the vessel.

options.label: Wait one more day for better cargo
options.consequence: You avoid a bad fixture, but the open position starts costing money and attention.

steps.title: How hard do you push the charter terms?
steps.prompt: The charterer wants broad weather exceptions and slow document flow. The owner wants a clean recap today.

options.label: Hold firm on demurrage and document deadlines
options.consequence: The recap is slower, but you buy clarity before the vessel starts moving.

options.label: Accept soft terms to close fast
options.consequence: The fixture closes quickly. The weak clauses are now waiting for the first port delay.

options.label: Walk away unless the recap is clean
options.consequence: You protect the ship, but the owner sees another day idle.

steps.title: The captain asks for safer instructions
steps.prompt: A weather window is closing. The captain recommends a slower, safer approach. The charterer is already pushing laycan.

options.label: Support the captain and inform charterers early
options.consequence: You lose some commercial sharpness but prevent the conflict from becoming a surprise.

options.label: Press for speed to protect laycan
options.consequence: You may protect the date, but the captain now feels commercial pressure is overruling seamanship.

options.label: Ask for two ETA options with risk notes
options.consequence: You turn a conflict into a decision table. The owner and captain can both see the tradeoff.

steps.title: Bunker margin is tight
steps.prompt: A cheaper stem is available later, but deviation now would reduce operational risk. The owner dislikes extra cost.

options.label: Approve a small deviation for safer bunkers
options.consequence: The voyage loses some margin but avoids a fragile fuel plan.

options.label: Wait for the cheaper stem
options.consequence: The spreadsheet improves, but the captain has less room for weather and port changes.

options.label: Take minimum now and review after loading
options.consequence: You preserve optionality, but you must actively manage the next decision.

steps.title: A delay claim appears
steps.prompt: The charterer blames the vessel for lost time. Your documents are decent but not perfect.

options.label: Reject the claim aggressively
options.consequence: You look strong today but risk turning a manageable dispute into a relationship problem.

options.label: Offer a documented compromise
options.consequence: You give up a little money to preserve the counterparty and close the file cleanly.

options.label: Escalate immediately to lawyers
options.consequence: You protect the formal position, but cost and delay rise before the facts are fully organized.

steps.title: What do you do after discharge?
steps.prompt: The vessel can chase another marginal cargo, reposition for a cleaner market, or pause for maintenance.

options.label: Chain another marginal cargo quickly
options.consequence: The ship stays busy, but accumulated operational risk follows you.

options.label: Reposition toward a cleaner market
options.consequence: You accept a quieter short-term result to improve the next negotiation.

options.label: Create a short maintenance window
options.consequence: The owner feels the cost, but the captain gets a vessel that can actually perform.
